practices: The AP Economics skills framework is organized into four categories. Skill Category 1, 'Principles and Models,' involves defining and describing economic concepts (30-40% of multiple-choice questions). Skill Category 2, 'Interpretation,' focuses on explaining given economic outcomes (25-32% of MCQs). Skill Category 3, 'Manipulation,' requires students to determine the outcomes of specific economic situations and the effects of changes (30-40% of MCQs). Skill Category 4, 'Graphing and Visuals,' involves modeling economic situations using graphs and is assessed in the free-response section (30-50% of FRQ points). Numerical analysis, which involves calculations, is integrated across the first three skills and accounts for 16-20% of total multiple-choice questions.

keyTopics: Scarcity and economic resources
Opportunity Cost and the Production Possibilities Curve (PPC)
Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade
Demand and the law of demand
Supply and the law of supply
Market Equilibrium, Disequilibrium, and Changes in Equilibrium

units.unitTitle: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle
keyTopics: The Circular Flow and Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
Limitations of GDP
Unemployment (frictional, structural, cyclical, and the natural rate)
Price Indices (CPI) and Inflation
Costs of Inflation
Real vs. Nominal GDP and the GDP Deflator
Business Cycles, including phases and turning points
Aggregate Demand (AD) and its determinants
Expenditure and Tax Multipliers
Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS)
Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS)
Equilibrium in the Aggregate Demand–Aggregate Supply (AD–AS) Model
Short-Run and Long-Run Self-Adjustment
Fiscal Policy actions and their effects
Automatic Stabilizers
Financial Assets (money, stocks, bonds)
Nominal vs. Real Interest Rates
Definition, Measurement, and Functions of Money (M1, M2)
Banking and the Expansion of the Money Supply, including the money multiplier
The Money Market
Monetary Policy and the tools of the central bank
The Loanable Funds Market
Fiscal and Monetary Policy Actions in the Short Run
The Phillips Curve (short-run and long-run)
Money Growth and Inflation (Quantity Theory of Money)
Government Deficits and the National Debt
Crowding Out
Economic Growth and the Aggregate Production Function
Public Policy and its effect on Economic Growth (e.g., supply-side policies)
Balance of Payments Accounts (Current and Capital/Financial)
Exchange Rates (Appreciation and Depreciation)
The Foreign Exchange Market
Effects of policies and economic conditions on the Foreign Exchange Market
Changes in the Foreign Exchange Market and Net Exports
Real Interest Rates and International Capital Flows

examFormat.freeResponse: 3 questions (1 long-form, 2 short-form), 60 minutes (includes a 10-minute reading period), 33.35% of score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly states that calculating the producer price index (PPI) is beyond the scope of the course and AP Exam. The course de-emphasizes rote memorization of specific economic data in favor of understanding and applying economic models. Students should avoid common mistakes such as conflating the market supply and demand model with the aggregate demand-aggregate supply model (e.g., by mislabeling the axes as 'Price' and 'Quantity' instead of 'Price Level' and 'Real GDP'). Additionally, when answering policy questions, students should identify the specific, appropriate policy action rather than listing all possible actions.
